draft.introduction: The Great Hymn to Osiris (c. 1550 BCE) on the Stele of Amenmose is the most complete surviving narrative of the Osiris myth in Egyptian. It recounts Osiris’s kingship, murder by Seth, reconstitution by Isis, and vindication through his son Horus.

translation.text: Hail to you, Osiris, Lord of Eternity, King of the Gods, of many names and holy forms, of secret rites in the temples. He is the noble ba at the head of Busiris, lord of abundance in Abydos.
translation.original_text: jnd-ḥr=k Wsjr nb ḏ.t nswt nṬr.w, āšā(.w) rn.w, sštā(.w) jrw m ḥw.wt-nṬr

translator_notes.note: Osiris’s title “Lord of Eternity” (nb ḏ.t) and his death-and-resurrection narrative made him the closest Egyptian parallel to Christ in early Christian comparative religion. The Church Fathers were aware of the parallel; Firmicus Maternus specifically attacked the Osiris cult as a demonic counterfeit.
